author: Tertullian
note: The earliest developed statement of the Trinity — one God distributed into a Trinity of three Persons 'of one substance, and of one condition, and of one power' (c. AD 210). DELICACY (flagged per selection rule): the antithetical clause 'three, however, not in condition, but in degree... not in power, but in aspect' uses 'in degree' (Lat. gradu) and, lifted in isolation, could be misread as subordinationist. It is NOT: the sentence is deliberately antithetical and RESOLVES to full co-equality — the distinction is expressly NOT of substance, condition, or power, but only of order/manifestation, 'inasmuch as He is one God.' The passage is quoted whole (with the resolving clause) precisely so the orthodox sense stands; do not excerpt only the 'in degree' half. An orthonote clarifier is advisable if this is surfaced in published content. Ellipsis marks an omitted 'dispensation/oikonomia' clause; both quoted segments are verbatim ANF. Translator = Dr. (Peter) Holmes. verified verbatim, incl. em-dash spacing (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: We, however, as we indeed always have done (and more especially since we have been better instructed by the Paraclete, who leads men indeed into all truth), believe that there is one only God ... placing in their order the three Persons—the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost: three, however, not in condition, but in degree; not in substance, but in form; not in power, but in aspect; yet of one substance, and of one condition, and of one power, inasmuch as He is one God, from whom these degrees and forms and aspects are reckoned, under the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost.

author: Tertullian
note: The famous 'semen est sanguis Christianorum' — 'the blood of Christians is seed' — persecution multiplies rather than extinguishes the church (c. AD 197). Some ANF reprints read 'the blood of the Christians is seed'; verify the presence/absence of the article and the exact clause 'the more in number we grow' vs 'we spring up in greater numbers' against source_url. Translator = S. Thelwall. verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: Nor does your cruelty, however exquisite, avail you; it is rather a temptation to us. The oftener we are mown down by you, the more in number we grow; the blood of Christians is seed.

author: Tertullian
note: 'What indeed has Athens to do with Jerusalem?' — the classic statement contrasting Greek philosophy and the Church (c. AD 200). CONTEXT NOTE for any use: this is a rhetorical warning against heresy-breeding speculation, NOT a blanket condemnation of reason or philosophy (Tertullian himself argues philosophically throughout); do not deploy it as an anti-intellectual proof-text. The companion 'rule of faith' passage in ch. 13 was also fetched cleanly and could be added as a separate entry if desired. Translator = Peter Holmes. verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: What indeed has Athens to do with Jerusalem? What concord is there between the Academy and the Church? What between heretics and Christians?

author: Tertullian
note: The pagans' remark 'See how they love one another' — external testimony to the visible love of the early Christian community (c. AD 197). Translator = S. Thelwall. verified verbatim, incl. quotation punctuation and 'will sooner put to death' (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: But it is mainly the deeds of a love so noble that lead many to put a brand upon us. See, they say, how they love one another, for themselves are animated by mutual hatred; how they are ready even to die for one another, for they themselves will sooner put to death.

author: Tertullian
note: The reality of the incarnation against docetism — 'it is by all means to be believed, because it is absurd' / 'the fact is certain, because it is impossible' (c. AD 206). Often loosely cited as 'credo quia absurdum,' a formula Tertullian never wrote; the ANF text is the accurate source. The point is not irrationalism but that the offense/scandal of a genuinely crucified, buried, and risen Son of God is itself evidence of a real event no one would invent — quote it with that sense, not as a boast against reason. Translator = Dr. (Peter) Holmes. verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: The Son of God was crucified; I am not ashamed because men must needs be ashamed of it. And the Son of God died; it is by all means to be believed, because it is absurd. And He was buried, and rose again; the fact is certain, because it is impossible.

author: Tertullian
note: The 'testimonium animae naturaliter Christianae' — the soul, in its spontaneous exclamations ('God is great,' 'God will judge'), bears unwitting witness to the one God (c. AD 197). ANF/Thelwall renders it 'O noble testimony of the soul by nature Christian!'; other editions read 'the soul naturally Christian' — confirm exact wording against source_url. Translator = S. Thelwall. verified verbatim (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: O noble testimony of the soul by nature Christian! Then, too, in using such words as these, it looks not to the Capitol, but to the heavens.
